segments.text: Welcome to our lecture on distributed systems fundamentals. In this lecture, we will cover some of the key concepts in distributed systems, including fault tolerance, consistency, and reliability.
Distributed systems must be able to tolerate failures in order to maintain availability. This involves detecting faults, tolerating failures, and responding to them.
Distributed systems must ensure consistency in order to maintain data integrity. This involves ensuring that all nodes agree on the state of the system.
Distributed systems must ensure reliability in order to maintain trust. This involves ensuring that data is durable and available.
Consensus algorithms are used to ensure consistency in distributed systems. There are two main types: leader-based and peer-to-peer.
Fault-tolerant systems use tools like ZooKeeper to ensure that the system can continue to operate even in the presence of failures.
Distributed file systems are used to store and manage large amounts of data. HDFS is a popular choice for distributed file systems.
MapReduce is just one tool in the toolbox for distributed systems. Other tools like Pig and Hive provide higher-level abstractions for processing data.
Message queuing is used to ensure that messages are delivered reliably. This involves buffering and queuing messages in order to ensure delivery.
